Question: Find the linear speed of a point on the equator of the earth if the earth has a radius of 3,960 miles and the earth rotates on its axis every 24 hours. Express answer in miles per hour.
o3 Answer:
Linear (tangential) speed v of a point on a rotating circle is  

v = arc-length traveled per unit time  
  = (radius)(angular displacement) / time  
  = rθ / t.

For one complete rotation of the earth  

• Radius r = 3,960 mi  
• Angular displacement θ = 2π rad (360°)  
• Time t = 24 h.

v = rθ/t  
  = (3,960 mi)(2π rad)/(24 h)  
  = 3,960(2π)/24 mi/h  
  = 3,960(π/12) mi/h  
  ≈ 3,960(0.261799) mi/h  
  ≈ 1,036.8 mi/h.

Rounded to a sensible number of significant digits:  

Linear speed at the equator ≈ 1.0 × 10³ mi/h, i.e. about 1,040 miles per hour.

• the ladder (length = 15 ft) – this is the hypotenuse  
• the ground – one leg  
• the wall – the vertical leg we want to find (call it h)

The angle between the ground and the ladder is 70°.  
Relative to this 70° angle:

opposite side = height h  
hypotenuse = 15 ft  

Use the sine function:

sin θ = (opposite)/(hypotenuse)

sin 70° = h / 15

h = 15 sin 70°

sin 70° ≈ 0.9397

h ≈ 15 × 0.9397 ≈ 14.1 ft

Therefore, the ladder reaches about 14 ft up the side of the building (to the nearest tenth of a foot, 14.1 ft).
